3A_Bibliographic.citation: Chu, J., Ganson, K. T., Baker, F. C., Testa, A., Jackson, D. B., Murray, S. B., & Nagata, J. M. (2023). Screen time and suicidal behaviors among U.S. children 9-11 years old: A prospective cohort study. Preventive Medicine, 169, 107452.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We used data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, which follows 11,875 children recruited from 21 diverse sites around the US... Specifically, we analyzed baseline (2016-2018, 9-10-years-old) and two-year-follow-up (2018-2020, 11-14-years-old) data from the ABCD 4.0 release. We excluded participants with missing data for screen time at baseline and suicidal behaviors at baseline and two-year follow-up.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Table 1 header says N=11,363 but abstract and text say N=11,633

3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, television shows/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social networking
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Participants answered questions about their typical screen time use through the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey, based on previously validated measures... Modalities included viewing/streaming TV shows or movies, watching/streaming videos, playing video games, texting, video chatting, and social media. Similar to a previous ABCD study, we performed a weighted average calculation of typical screen time as follows: ((weekday average x 5) + (weekend average x 2))/7. The weighted average for each modality was then reported as a continuous variable.

3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Suicidal behaviors were assessed using the Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5), a computerized tool based on the DSM-5. Adolescents completed all modules of the KSADS-5 to reflect mental health symptoms and diagnoses. Participants who reported passive suicidal ideation, nonspecific active suicidal ideation, active suicidal ideation with a plan/method/preparation/intent, or suicide attempt were coded as having suicidal behaviors.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple logistic regression with propensity weights
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: race/ethnicity, sex, household income, parent education, site, baseline major depressive disorder, baseline suicidal behaviors, family history of psychiatric disorders
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple logistic regression analyses were conducted in 2022 using Stata 15.1 (StataCorp, College Station, TX) to estimate associations between baseline screen time (exposure) and suicidal behaviors at two-year-follow-up (outcome), adjusting for confounders. In sensitivity analyses excluding participants with suicidal behaviors at baseline, findings were unchanged. Propensity weights were applied to yield representative estimates based on the American Community Survey from the US Census.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: each additional hour of total screen time at baseline was prospectively associated with 1.09 higher odds of reporting suicidal behaviors at two-year-follow-up (95% confidence interval [CI] 1.03-1.14)
Table 2: Television shows/movies adjusted OR 1.10 (0.93-1.30), p=0.251
Table 2: Videos (e.g., YouTube) adjusted OR 1.21 (1.04-1.39), p=0.012
Table 2: Video games adjusted OR 1.18 (1.01-1.38), p=0.036
each additional hour of texting (aOR 1.36, 95% CI 1.06-1.74)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Adjusted OR per hour/day; not significant
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: Social networking adjusted OR 1.27 (0.97-1.66), p=0.076

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Effect sizes of the associations were relatively small. However, they are reported for each hour per day; therefore, total effects are greater with higher screen time.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper acknowledges small effect sizes in limitations but then uses per-unit accumulation argument to override the concern.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Health care providers may consider assessing screen use when evaluating adolescents for suicidal behaviors. Professional organizations such as the American Academy of Pediatrics should provide specific guidance for families regarding screen time usage and strategies to reduce suicidal behaviors in children and adolescents.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: Effect sizes of the associations were relatively small. However, they are reported for each hour per day; therefore, total effects are greater with higher screen time.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames the prospective association between screen time and suicidal behaviors as consistent with prior literature and discusses potential mechanisms (cyberbullying, graphic content exposure, sedentary behavior). It acknowledges small effect sizes but uses per-unit framing to suggest cumulative impact. Despite noting cross-sectional limitations and the inability to establish causality, it calls for clinician screening and AAP guidance on screen time to reduce suicidal behaviors.
